editorial.title: The green street dmz: horticultural hegemony vs. The nicotine cloud
editorial.teaser: Forget geopolitics; the true face of modern warfare is currently being drafted in a suburban front yard involving prize-winning hydrangeas and a relentless barrage of marlboro golds.
editorial.body:
If you wish to study the decline of western civilization, simply pull up a lawn chair on green street. Here, a microscopic yet vicious turf war has erupted that makes the fall of rome look like a minor hoa dispute.

in the red corner, we have mrs. Elspeth vance, a woman who prunes her roses with the surgical precision of a neurosurgeon and views a stray dandelion as a personal insult from god.

in the grey, hazy corner, we have "davvo" from number 42, a man whose lung capacity is a medical marvel and whose porch-smoking habits are as predictable as the tides. The conflict began, as all great wars do, with a boundary dispute.

davvo, employing a flick of the wrist that suggests a complete detachment from societal consequences, launched a spent cigarette butt over the fence. It landed, tragically, amidst elspeth’s prize-winning 'incrediball' hydrangeas.

the retaliation was swift and disproportionate. Elspeth didn't just return the butt; she returned it inside a ziplock bag stapled to a laminated letter citing council by-laws regarding littering and "moral decay." davvo responded by moving his smoking chair three inches closer to the fence line and chain-smoking like a noir detective on a deadline.

we are now in the escalation phase. Elspeth has installed motion-activated sprinklers calibrated not for hydration, but for sniper-like soakings of anyone leaning over the railing.

davvo has countered with a high-powered industrial fan, blowing a steady stream of smoke directly into her petunias, essentially gassing her begonias into submission. The neighborhood watches in horror.

we aren't worried about the property values; we’re worried about the inevitable escalation to biological warfare involving fermented fertilizer and burning tyres. On green street, the grass isn't greener; it’s just heavily fortified.
editorial.author: Professor Remy
